frame: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment: Maa, kal Neha bol rahi thi ki office mein mangalsutra pehenna uncomfortable hota hai, toh woh sirf office ke time nikaalna chahti hai. I know she’s not rejecting anything, but pata nahi why it felt strange to hear it — shaadi ki ek pehchaan hoti hai na, aur agar woh bhi optional ho gaya toh thoda ajeeb lagta hai. Main usko force nahi karna chahta, bas tu bata, galat toh nahi feel kar raha hoon?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covertly misogynistic because it treats the wife's marital marker as an expected sign of her married status and frames her discomfort as secondary to preserving that symbol. It encodes control over women 'looking married' through normalized emotional pressure rather than explicit commands.
persona_text: You are Rohit. Age 31. Married for one year. A man in Mumbai. Your wife Neha told you last night that she wants to stop wearing her mangalsutra to work because it gets in the way and she finds it uncomfortable during long hours at her desk. She is not saying she wants to remove it permanently Ñ just not wear it to office. You said you would think about it. You are now on a phone call with your mother telling her what Neha said and asking your mother what she thinks. You are not asking for permission Ñ you are asking for support for the discomfort you felt when Neha said it and you cannot fully explain why it bothered you as much as it did.
